name: AI Competitive Intelligence Digest - Alba Corp

options.systemMessage:
You are a Competitive Intelligence Analyst embedded in OpenAI's strategy team.

You are handed ONE raw update about a competitor. Assess it the way an analyst briefing the executive team would: skeptical, specific, and willing to say something is unimportant.

Rules:
- Judge the item on its merits. Most updates are routine. Do not inflate a minor blog post into a strategic threat.
- "Business impact" means impact on OpenAI's revenue, moat, or roadmap - not a restatement of the headline.
- "Customer impact" means what an OpenAI enterprise or API customer would notice or care about.
- threat_level and opportunity_level are integers 1-10. A typical item is 2-4. Reserve 8+ for genuine strategic events (frontier model launch, major pricing move, large enterprise defection).
- confidence is 1-10 and reflects how much signal the source text actually contains. Thin or headline-only sources should score low.
- recommended_action must be a concrete next step someone could assign, not "monitor the situation".
- executive_summary is ONE sentence, under 25 words, written for a reader who will read nothing else.

Return only the structured object. No preamble, no markdown.

parameters.jsonSchemaExample:
{
  "category": "model_launch",
  "summary": "Two to three sentences describing what actually happened.",
  "business_impact": "How this affects OpenAI's revenue, moat, or roadmap.",
  "customer_impact": "What an OpenAI customer would notice or care about.",
  "sentiment": "positive",
  "threat_level": 7,
  "opportunity_level": 3,
  "priority": "High",
  "recommended_action": "A concrete, assignable next step.",
  "confidence": 8,
  "executive_summary": "One sentence under 25 words."
}

parameters.content:
## 1. Collect

Every morning at 9, we go looking for news about six competitors.

For each company we check four places: their own blog, their GitHub
account, Hacker News, and Google News. We only keep things published
in the last day or two — old news isn't news.

Websites go down and feeds break. So if one source fails, we try it
three times, then give up on that one source and carry on with the
rest. A dead blog feed for one company never stops the other five
from being checked. Anything that failed gets written to the
error_log sheet so you can see what broke and fix it.

## 2. Dedupe & Idempotency

The same story shows up in lots of places. We only want to read
about it once.

First we throw away exact repeats. Then we check the "seen ledger" —
a sheet listing every story we've already reported on. Anything
already in that list gets dropped.

That's what makes this safe to re-run. If you run the workflow five
times today, you get one email. The first run reports the news, the
next four find nothing new and quietly stop.

Note: we don't match stories by their web link. Google News gives
every article a scrambled link, so the same story arrives with
different links each time. We match on the headline and the
publisher instead, which actually works.

## 3. Analyse

Each new story goes to Gemini, which reads it as if it were a
competitive analyst sitting inside OpenAI.

For every story it answers: what happened, what this means for our
business, what our customers would notice, how threatening it is
(1-10), how much opportunity it holds (1-10), how confident it is in
its own read, and what someone should actually do about it.

Two safety nets:

The AI does NOT decide priority. It scores things; a set of fixed
rules decides what counts as Critical or High. That way "Critical"
means the same thing every day, even if the AI has an off morning.
Those rules live in the Validate AI Output node and you can read
them without being a programmer.

If the AI returns something broken or empty, we don't crash. That
story goes through as a low-confidence placeholder and the run
continues.

## 4. Deliver

Every branch comes back together at the Merge node, so everything
travels onward as one batch. This is why you get one email instead
of one per priority level.

Each story is saved to two sheets: "history" for the record, and
"seen ledger" so tomorrow's run knows to skip it. This happens
before the email is built — deliberately. If the email fails to
send, tomorrow's run still won't repeat today's stories.

Then everything is gathered into a single report, formatted as
HTML, and emailed. Stories are grouped Critical first, then High,
Medium and Low, with the recommended actions collected at the
bottom.

If the email itself fails, that failure gets logged too, so a
silent inbox never goes unexplained.
Please update your respective email to test

## What this workflow does

We are OpenAI. This workflow watches our competitors for us.

Every weekday morning at 9am it checks what six rival AI labs have
been doing, works out which of it actually matters to us, and emails
the strategy team a single briefing.

Who we're watching:

  Anthropic          Claude models — our closest rival on frontier
                     model quality and enterprise API business
  Google DeepMind    Gemini — backed by Google's distribution and
                     compute
  xAI                Grok — bundled into X and increasingly into
                     other products
  Perplexity         AI search — competes for the consumer
                     question-answering use case
  Mistral AI         Open-weight European models — competes on cost
                     and sovereignty
  Cohere             Enterprise and RAG-focused — competes for the
                     same business customers

Everything the AI reads is judged from OpenAI's point of view:
does this threaten our revenue, our technical lead, or our
customers? A competitor's routine blog post is not news. A
competitor matching our flagship model at half the price is.

To change who we watch, edit the Build Competitor Registry node.
That list is the only place competitors are defined — nothing else
in the workflow needs touching.
